data.title: ClearPoint Neuro: Temporary Headwinds Don't Change The Long-Term Outlook
data.description:
ClearPoint Neuro's (CLPT) stock has fallen significantly in early 2026, driven by weak financial guidance and increased uncertainty regarding the FDA's attitude towards gene therapies for rare diseases. Softer growth expectations for 2026 are largely the result of the preclinical CRO facility launch pushing out and reduced expectations for revenue from biopharma partners' commercializing their therapies.

I previously suggested that ClearPoint was positioned for robust growth in 2026, driven by IRRAS, the PRISM 1.5T system clearance, and the company's new CRO facility. The stock is down around 17% since then, although it dropped as much as 40%.

Current concerns are short-sighted, though, as ClearPoint has a strong competitive position, and a step change in revenue remains likely. While AMT-130's near-term fate remains uncertain, Parkinson's and epilepsy partners are far more important to ClearPoint's future. These programs are in Phase III trials at the moment and do not have the same trial design concerns as rare disease partner programs. It may make time, but I continue to believe that ClearPoint's stock will move significantly higher within the next few years.

Market Conditions

The most important market developments for ClearPoint in recent months relate to the FDA's attitude towards rare disease therapies. Regenxbio's gene therapy for Hunter syndrome was rejected by the FDA in February, with the use of a natural history control arm and a biomarker as a surrogate endpoint cited as concerns. MPS II is a rare condition, meaning any associated revenue would likely be inconsequential for ClearPoint. The decision is indicative of the regulatory environment, though.

There was also a fairly public spat over uniQure's (QURE) trial design and the quality of the data generated. By March, the FDA was insisting on a randomized, double-blind, sham surgery–controlled. While this may be scientific best practice, in this scenario it is highly unethical and is not a realistic option.

Soon after these events, Vinay Prasad announced that he was leaving the FDA, which suggests that pressure is mounting on the FDA to provide more regulatory flexibility for rare disease treatments. It remains to be seen whether a new CBER head will be sufficient to provide uniQure and Regenxbio with a more viable path to commercialization though.

Regardless, ClearPoint's future is more likely to hinge on the outcomes of the Phase III trials being conducted by BlueRock Therapeutics and Neurona. The first patient in BlueRock's Phase III Parkinson's trial was treated in September 2025. This is a double-blind, sham-surgery-controlled study enrolling around 100 patients, with data expected in 2027.

The first participants in Neurona's Phase III epilepsy trial are expected to be treated in the second half of 2026, with data available around the end of 2029. Again, this is a relatively large, sham-controlled study, reducing concerns about data quality. Data generated so far continues to suggest that NRTX-1001 is both safe and effective.

Beyond rare diseases, Parkinson's, and epilepsy, there is still a significant opportunity. More than 30 million people in the US suffer from severe neurological disorders, and 2.1 million of these patients have disorders where a ClearPoint BioPharma partner has already been accepted for FDA expedited review. ClearPoint has more than 60 active biopharma partners and is participating in more than 25 active clinical trials covering over 15 indications. Less than 300,000 people have been treated with minimally invasive neurosurgery so far, though, raising questions about adoption and scalability.

ClearPoint is targeting a 20% share of a 1 billion USD global opportunity, with commercial biologicals and drug delivery potentially increasing this opportunity significantly. While there is uncertainty about ClearPoint's ability to hit this target, it highlights the size of the opportunity beyond commercial drug delivery.

ClearPoint Business Updates

ClearPoint now segments its business as follows:

  • Drug delivery products and services
  • Neurosurgery navigation and robotics
  • Laser therapy and access
  • Neurocritical management

Within its drug delivery business, ClearPoint is developing a range of solutions that will support development and its competitive positioning:

  • Brain segmentation tools
  • Predictive drug delivery modeling
  • Preplanning and navigation software
  • Frame and robotics delivery options
  • Drug loading and mechanized infusion technologies
  • Post-procedure quality confirmation software

The ClearPoint Advanced Laboratories facility in California performed its first preclinical study in the fourth quarter of 2025. While ClearPoint can now do smaller studies, the actual launch of the facility isn't expected until the second half of 2026. This will enable ClearPoint to perform GLP studies and cell culture. PET / SPECT imaging is anticipated in the first half of 2027, and ClearPoint will introduce a bioanalytic and pathology lab in the second half of 2027.

ClearPoint has also initiated the PMDA regulatory process in Japan at the request of a number of its partners. The company expects to perform its first cell therapy infusion in Japan some time in the second half of the year.

ClearPoint's robotics platform is progressing, and the company expects to perform product usability showcases this year. ClearPoint also wants to perform its first preclinical studies with the robotic platform later in the year. The robotic system isn't expected to be introduced until 2028 or later, though.

Adeor recently received FDA clearance for the Velocity Alpha MR conditional power drill, and the first clinical procedure was performed with the drill. The drill is currently in limited market release, although it has little importance from a revenue perspective. Rather, it is significant due to its potential to reduce procedure times and improve scalability.

IRRAS continues to enhance its solution and is generating clinical data in support of expansion. The business already appears to be facing headwinds, though, with ClearPoint undertaking a strategic reset. ClearPoint has stated that it is rationalizing the distributors that it is working with, and this appears set to weigh in on near-term growth. Whether it is indicative of larger problems is unclear, though.

Financial Analysis

ClearPoint generated 10.4 million USD in revenue in the fourth quarter, an increase of roughly 33% YoY. Biologics and drug delivery growth was driven by product sales as partners advanced development programs. Service revenue was down modestly, though. Neurosurgery Navigation and therapy growth was strong, due in large part to the contribution from IRRAS (~1.2 million USD in a little over a month). Capital equipment revenue was soft, which was disappointing given ClearPoint's efforts to expand its footprint.

ClearPoint expects 52-56 million USD revenue in 2026, down from prior guidance of 54-56 million USD that was given only a few weeks earlier. A substantial portion of this likely relates to lower expectations for biopharma partner revenue given recent regulatory developments. IRRAS integration efforts also appear to have led ClearPoint to reassess near-term revenue expectations. Double-digit revenue growth is expected from each of the company's 4 product lines, though. Looking further ahead, ClearPoint has suggested that 15-20% annual growth is possible, independent of the commercialization of partner therapies.

ClearPoint will likely release its second-quarter earnings in the next few weeks. Growth is likely to remain robust, despite the fact that the 2025 comparable period is significantly more difficult. This largely stems from the IRRAS acquisition, with organic growth likely to land in the low double digits. Capital Equipment sales and navigation revenue will be items to watch, as will be the performance of IRRAS.

ClearPoint's gross margin was 62% in the fourth quarter, while operating expenses totaled 13.4 million USD. The YoY increase in operating expenses was driven by the IRRAS acquisition and professional services fees. ClearPoint is also expanding its commercial organization and incurred severance expenses associated with the IRRAS acquisition in Q4. While losses and cash burn are still elevated, ClearPoint has close to 50 million USD in cash, which likely provides sufficient runway until biopharma partner commercial revenue begins to ramp.

Risks

As a cash burning business that is still trying to establish itself, ClearPoint is a high risk investment, despite the long-term potential. This is reflected in the high short interest, which likely stems from an expectation that the drug delivery business will never live up to expectations. While more partner FDA approvals are needed to establish the long-term viability of this business, Neurona and BlueRock Therapeutics will not face the same issues that rare disease partners have.

Navigation and laser therapy growth has also been somewhat disappointing recently, and the IRRAS already appears to be falling short of expectations. ClearPoint's long-term market share targets suggest that this is just due to temporary headwinds, but the company really needs to start generating more consistent growth.

Conclusion

ClearPoint's 2026 guidance is weak in light of expected tailwinds from the IRRAS acquisition, the rollout of the PRISM Laser Therapy System with 1.5T MRI guidance, and the launch of ClearPoint's CRO facility. As a result, it is not surprising that ClearPoint's share price has come under pressure in recent months. A situation that has been exacerbated by the FDA's current reluctance to approve rare disease therapies.

After the recent share price decline and the IRRAS acquisition, ClearPoint's forward EV/S is probably only around 6. This is fairly reasonable given ClearPoint's growth target and likely margins at scale, independent of the step change in revenue that will come from the commercialization of partner therapies. As a result, I continue to believe that ClearPoint's stock will move significantly higher at some point in the next few years, contingent on Parkinson's and epilepsy partner programs continuing to progress towards commercialization.

data.title: The Coiled Spring Effect: Why Modine's Gross Profit Signals Explosive Stock Upside
data.description:
Market participants now understand why the shares of memory manufacturers, such as Sandisk (SNDK) or Micron (MU), showed an extraordinary rally. The construction boom of data centers to meet the needs of artificial intelligence created a severe deficit of high-speed memory. However, those investors who earned substantial returns on this trend opened their positions in that very moment, when the connection between advanced AI and boring manufacturers of flash drives was not yet obvious to the broad public.

And I think the situation repeats itself.

Right now a similar picture is unfolding around Modine Manufacturing (MOD). This century-old engineering company specializes in systems of heat exchange and thermal regulation. Simply put, they make industrial cooling. And exactly this, it would seem, prosaic segment becomes the new field of a technological battle.

The investment cycle of artificial intelligence is not monolithic. It develops in waves. The development and huge demand in different segments of the economy go strictly by turn, creating a sequential market dynamic. First arose a colossal demand for developers of base models and software, such as Microsoft (MSFT), OpenAI, and Google (GOOGL). Behind them followed the second stage, when unprecedented computing capacities and chips became necessary to the industry, which elevated NVIDIA (NVDA) and AMD (AMD) to the summit. The third phase led to the takeoff of companies responsible for the storage and transfer of huge arrays of data—those very Sandisk and Arista Networks (ANET).

It is fully logical to assume that the next, fourth wave of this technological expansion inevitably will reach physical infrastructure. We are talking about energy supply and thermal regulation. If NVIDIA acts as a peculiar brain of artificial intelligence and Sandisk works as its memory, then the systems from Modine can be called its system of life support. As data centers transition to chips of a new generation, of the level of Blackwell and higher, the emission of heat reaches truly critical values. Traditional air conditioners physically do not cope with the heat dissipation from racks, the power of which exceeds 100 kW.

The industry needs liquid cooling.

It ceased to be simply an interesting option for enthusiasts. This technology fast turned into a harsh technological requirement and the new bottleneck of the whole branch, where the engineering experience of Modine comes to the forefront.

From Radiators for Cars to AI Servers

The transformation of the business from radiators for cars to AI servers is in full swing. But many to this day classify Modine as a supplier of components for the automotive and heavy industry, since historically the company indeed made heat exchangers for trucks and special machinery. But a look under the hood shows a radical shift of paradigm in their business model.

The leadership brilliantly realizes a strategy of focusing. They methodically cut off low-margin contracts in the traditional transport segment. The freed capital by a powerful stream directs itself into the subdivision of climatic solutions and, concretely, into the development of the brand Airedale, specifically focused on the cooling of data centers.

The change of the economic paradigm is obvious. To realize the real potential of growth of Modine, it is necessary to clearly understand the cardinal difference in the economics of their old and new markets. In the automotive industry, a radiator is a commodity of mass consumption. Auto concerns there harshly press suppliers, squeezing out literally every penny from their margin. In a data center, the system of cooling appears already a critical infrastructure.

The price of a mistake here is astronomical.

If the cooling fails in a computing cluster on a hundred thousand chips, an operator of the level of Amazon (AMZN) or Google will lose hundreds of millions of dollars over counted hours of downtime and damage of ultra-expensive equipment. In the segment of artificial intelligence, clients buy not simply hardware. They pay for the guarantee of uninterrupted work. Exactly this fact allows Modine to dictate completely other, much more attractive prices. The financial reporting directly confirms this thesis, showing that the organic growth of sales of equipment for centers of processing of data constituted a staggering 78% year over year by the fiscal year results of the third quarter.

The company does not simply grow revenue. It qualitatively changes itself, methodically raising the technological complexity and value of its product.

Finances

The financial divergence creates a classic effect of a coiled spring. A superficial analysis of multiples can give a distorted picture and scare off an unsophisticated investor. The capitalization of the company exceeded 13 billion dollars. Revenue stably grows. However, net profit for now seems disproportionately small, due to which the classic coefficient P/E looks non-standard and does not serve as a representative indicator of the absolute health of the business.

The real picture uncovers itself only at a deep analysis of the dynamic of gross profit.

The analysis of this divergence answers the main question about where the money goes. The data in these tables indicates a clear financial anomaly. Revenue from the 2021–2022 years grew approximately by 30–40%, but gross profit for that very same period flew up by more than 100%, having reached an impressive mark of 645 million dollars by the fiscal year results of the 2025 year. This appears to be a direct mathematical proof of that fact, that the margin of new high-tech contracts for data centers multiply exceeds the margin of the old automotive business.

A reasonable question arises about why this powerful cash flow from gross profit for now does not hit the bottom line. The answer hides in an aggressive cycle of capital expenses.

Modine is located in a stage of massive re-equipment. Hundreds of millions of dollars right now are invested into the expansion and launch of new factories for the production of precision chillers in Wisconsin, Virginia, and Great Britain. The amortization of these large objects and colossal expenses on their launch naturally presses on current incomes—which is well visible by the net loss in the last quarter. Besides that, the active preparation for the divestiture of the old automotive business carries with it essential one-time legal and operational expenses.

Investors often confuse investments into absolute market leadership in a new market with banal operational inefficiency.

This may be a misinterpretation of the company's structural pivot.

A robust gross profit is already being generated by the very core of the business. As soon as the peak of capital expenses will be safely passed in the nearest years, and the one-time costs of restructuring will disappear from the reporting balances, hundreds of millions of dollars of incremental gross profit will begin to directly flow over into net profit. But here hides a most important nuance—we deal with a double potential growth in the future. Current investments into the rebuilding of factories inevitably will lead to the company being able to produce and deliver significantly more high-margin production for data centers. That is, a powerful organic growth of the very volumes of sales will add itself to the mathematical growth of net profit at the expense of the finishing of infrastructural constructions.

Unconditionally, the shares of the company already showed impressive growth recently. However, the current quotes lay down only the preservation of actual volumes of realization, completely not accounting for this double effect from the coming scaling of production capacities. The built-in power of the business already today allows it to generate stable incomes. And these incomes in the foreseeable future will radically lower the forward multiples of the company, making the current valuation of its shares extremely attractive for an entry.

The Battle for Manufacturing Capacities

The battle for manufacturing capacities allows Modine to win precious time. In the current year, the market for the infrastructure of artificial intelligence is a harsh, uncompromising market for the seller. The problem of the industry right now is not about finding new clients. Hyperscalers literally stand in a queue with open checkbooks, and only the physical presence of equipment acts as the main obstacle.

A system of cooling is simply impossible to sell if you have nowhere to assemble it.

A real race for manufacturing capacity has begun. Competitors loudly announce the construction of mega-factories, off the bat investing hundreds of millions of dollars. However, the erection of a large enterprise as a greenfield project takes long years, depending on coordination, the building of logistics, the hiring of personnel, and complex commissioning. At technological giants, there are no these years in reserve.

New ultra-powerful chips are already being delivered today.

Here hides the key operational advantage of Modine. The company competently uses a tactic of internal repurposing. Possessing a huge global network of factories, which for decades uninterruptedly produced heat exchangers for automobiles, the leadership simply changed the profile of these working platforms. Old lines on the release of cheap radiators operatively wind down. On those very same squares, with already supplied megawatts of energy and trained technical personnel, the assembly of high-tech chillers rapidly unrolls.

This non-standard solution provides a phenomenal speed of exit onto the market. While direct competitors only pour a concrete foundation, ready systems of liquid cooling are already shipping in full swing from the repurposed factories, quickly absorbing the formed world market deficit.

Competitive Landscape

The competitive landscape of the market of cooling reminds one of a confrontation between a narrowly specialized engineering boutique and a huge infrastructural supermarket. Vertiv Holdings (VRT) today appears the unconditional leader of the market of data centers. This is a gigantic corporation, supplying practically all necessary equipment, beginning with server racks and sources of uninterrupted power and ending with systems of thermal regulation. The shares of Vertiv trade themselves with a huge premium, since the market highly values their scales. Also, diversified conglomerates like Schneider Electric (SBGSY) are present, for which complex systems of cooling remain only a small part of a huge empire of electrical technics.

The uniqueness of the approach of Modine consists exactly in focus. Through its brand, the company positions itself as a profile engineering center. Buying these shares, an investor makes a clean, not blurred, bet exactly on the solution of the approaching problem of the overheating of AI chips, not tying his capital with other sectors of the economy. Offering often superior technologies of direct heat dissipation, the company still trades itself with a significant discount by comparison with the recognized leaders of the sector.

This is a rare opportunity to buy high-paced technological growth at the price of an old industrial company. A narrow and successful specialization also makes this business a highly attractive candidate for a potential absorption from the side of larger players wishing momentarily to strengthen their positions.

Valuation

To properly assess Modine's upside, it is essential to look at normalized earnings power rather than temporary P/E distortions. Perspectives of scaling and a look into the year 2030 show that it is important to account for the long-term potential of growth, which for now is only partially reflected in the quotes. According to analyst forecasts, the global data center cooling market will reach the $42 billion mark by 2030, which practically means a doubling of current volumes. For Modine, this opens a direct path to a multiple increase of revenue in its most profitable segment.

If the company preserves its current dynamic of market share capture and scales its new capacity, its gross profit is projected to exceed the $1 billion mark. With the normalization of net margins as CAPEX subsides, this would allow Modine to reach a net profit level of $500–$700 million and higher. Applying a forward P/E multiple of 25x–30x—which remains conservative compared to the higher multiples often seen in pure-play infrastructure peers like Vertiv—results in a potential market capitalization significantly above current levels. Thus, even after the impressive rally of recent years, the shares preserve a fundamental potential for further revaluation. Furthermore, Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings currently highlight strong momentum and profitability metrics for Modine, further supporting the bullish thesis and signaling that systematic models are already recognizing this underlying strength.

Risks

The risks of an investment thesis always demand maximally sober valuation. A deep structural transformation inevitably accompanies itself by serious challenges. Against the background of an aggressive cycle of capital expenses, the net debt of the company grew, which tentatively reached the mark of 517 million dollars by the end of the past year. In the case of a sharp macroeconomic shock and a subsequent freezing of the construction of new data centers, the presence of unfinished manufacturing capacities and large debt obligations can become a serious problem against the background of a falling revenue.

A strong dependence on several of the world's largest hyperscalers also carries in itself obvious risks. These huge corporations possess a colossal negotiating power on the market. As soon as the acute deficit of manufacturing capacities subsides, they unfailingly will begin to harshly press on the margin of their suppliers. The process itself of the separation of the automotive business requires a filigree work of management, and any unforeseen legal delays can dangerously distract the leadership from the main focus.

Investors should also not discount the threat of overproduction on the horizon of the following several years. When all key players finally finish building their new factories, supply can exceed demand, which unavoidably will lead to exhausting price wars in the industry.

Conclusion

The valuation of the situation and future catalysts leads to a conclusion. The participants of the market often interpret the financial indicators of companies finding themselves in a stage of deep structural transformation with a delay. Current multiples and a highly modest, at first glance, net profit still reflect the profile of a standard manufacturer of auto parts. However, the stable growth of the segment of data centers by 78% directly and unambiguously points to one of the key beneficiaries of the development of the whole infrastructure of artificial intelligence.

Applying a standard sum-of-the-parts valuation method, fairly valuing the growing technological segment, and maximally conservatively valuing the automotive business, the real value of the shares will turn out significantly higher than their current quotes.

The quarterly reporting will become the next important catalyst, which is expected in the middle of May 2026. The basic focus at the breakdown of these figures will be directed not so much on the current profit per share as much as on the ability of the company to preserve the given paces of growth of gross profit and on the news about the terms of the completion of the separation of outdated subdivisions. As soon as the market conclusively realizes the fact that the high-margin business of the cooling of equipment became the new core of the company, the financial spring is positioned to uncoil.

Investments into manufacturing bases will turn into a real net profit. This naturally will lead to a recalculation of multiples by the standards of the high-tech sector. Modine Manufacturing is a high-quality and well-thought-out bet on the physical infrastructure of AI for those who seek confident growth at an adequate price.

Final rating: Buy.

data.title: Brown & Brown: No Significant Upside After 1Q26
data.description:
I don't take joy in being right at times. There's a lot to like about certain insurance companies. In this case, excellent organic growth history, an excellent "agency" sort of model, and improving business fundamentals could spell a positive picture for this company. However, at the same time, it's very clear that Brown & Brown (BRO) has not been in a good position to outperform for some time here, and I don't think the future will provide significant upside here either.

It's interesting because many analysts and investors are positive about BRO. Why is that?

Because of excellent revenue growth, a successful M&A strategy (largely speaking, at the very least), strong underlying operational cash flow and working margins. Many things, if you were to look at the company from a high level, imply that the company is likely to outperform here. If we combine this with proven, high degrees of insider ownership (above 16%), we have a high degree of interest alignment. A complete positive as well, for sure.

The ratings and suggestions here look like a traffic light - which might sound like a weird expression - so let me qualify that.

There you go - the traffic light. From "BUY" to "SELL", the entire spectrum (almost) is represented. Bulls tend to focus on the very good profitability of the company, which is where it wins - while bears, like myself, tend to focus on the overly high valuation for what's on offer (yes, even now), as well as the risk to momentum and growth, among other things-

There is definitely a price at which BRO is a buyable company; however, the fact that the quant rating puts this insurance broker nearly at the bottom percentile in the sector, industry, and overall, says at least something.

So let me update you on what I personally believe that it says here. As the single analyst on Seeking Alpha who has correctly predicted the declining rates vis-a-vis the rating I hold since about July of 2025, I believe I do have at least a bit of an idea of what's going on here.

Brown & Brown - Challenges for 2026 after 1Q26

Many analysts focus, for the positivity of the company, on the successful execution of its M&A's. This is entirely valid and a good perspective to have. The problem, as I made clear in my previous piece as well, is that the company needs to be priced not only for the upsides that this brings, but also for the risk - things like interest expense, and general overvaluation.

I also said that BRO has not always been this premiumized. COVID-19 is what brought this around. I will go on record here saying that BRO is a lot more attractive than 4 months ago. It's definitely approaching a valuation where I see something like an upside could be garnered - especially with its history of never negatively missing an earnings estimate, and often beating it.

But what still speaks against the company, as we've gotten 1Q26 are both fundamentals and sector trends. First off, BBB- is still there. The company has a poor valuation overall compared to other insurers, which often trade at 6-10x P/E. Even after the 20% drop, the company is above 15x P/E. The yield is about 1%, which is significantly low for insurance. Growing at about 7-9% per year, I do not see enough of an argument, either in fundamentals or earnings trend, to allow a significant 15x+ Premiumization for this business, which means that in any scenario I see as likely, 15x is as high as this can possibly go.

And if we use the 15x P/E estimate, you may, at this point, no longer lose money, but you're also not making more than 9% per year if these forecasts hold.

You may recall in my last article that I gave the company a target of $62/share. Unfortunately, my outlook for insurance and insurance brokers has worsened a lot since December of last year. The combination of softening markets, tech and AI, litigation in insurance in the US, which is this company's primary market, and higher compliance costs and regulatory costs, these things all speak of an environment that conveys growth challenges for an insurance business. BRO, which is growing organically as well as inorganically, cannot grow easily without either adding interest costs or raising premiums, something which is getting increasingly harder.

The market has softened a lot more, in fact, over the past 4 months. This does not speak favorably to these companies to even achieve its previously stated growth targets. The estimated growth targets for BRO confirm this. 4-6 months ago, when I wrote my last piece, BRO's estimated growth target was close to 10% on average. It was what guided my PT. Now it's less than 8.2% on average, and I think fundamental trends have worsened even more than that.

BRO is all about that valuation, and will continue to be so in 1Q26 as well. The challenges are already visible in the last results, if you know where to look. An organic revenue decrease, despite good M&A's and otherwise good trends.

This reflects the market where anything the company currently does that adds positive growth seems to be inorganic. I also believe, in that report, the company was being too optimistic for its estimated rates of revenue increase, especially when considering the drop in speciality distribution I 4Q.

These trends all speak of the same thing - a softening market. And the valuation for BRO does not yet reflect the full impact of this softening market, because the earnings growth and margins are hidden behind inorganic advantages. So I'm still not saying that BRO is in any way a bad business - I just think that it's priced incorrectly.

I believe this incorrect pricing can be seen in part of the sector itself, where few brokers trade as high, and where the market forecasts overall don't support the estimates given here. For 1Q26, consensus estimates was an EPS growth of about 5.4%, with revenue (inorganic) at over 30%, in line with 4Q, and an expectation I can confirm that I share. This expectation was, as of the 1Q26 report, also materialized.

My problem with these results is that the 5% AEPS growth is on an annualized basis, not even close to enough to support the company's 3-year AEPS estimate, and BRO is not a seasonal company - meaning there's no reason to expect a positive surprise in any of the quarters. If we look at the AH reaction to the results, the market with less than 1-2% valuation growth, also confirms this lack of excitement. Despite what bulls might argue is a very substantial growth of the top line and improvement to the bottom line, the stock does not seem to move much.

This means that the valuation targets that I gave, the assumptions I cited, and the industry fundamentals I showed in my last article are still very much valid. It means that the company's current forecast is as follows.

Brown & Brown Valuation - Lowering my PT, hoping for the company to break below $60/share

As I said above, I unfortunately need to cut my target to reflect the lower growth estimate the company is providing us with here, and 1Q26 does not change my estimate. Using 7-8% as opposed to 9-10% means that the $65/share doesn't work. Adding softening industry fundamentals to the equation means I'm also increasing my general discount from 14-15 to 13-14x P/E at most. This calls for a PT of at most $58/share, which is meaningfully below the current level, and meaningfully below any level the company has been for some time. It dipped briefly to $63/share a few weeks back, but I still didn't consider it attractive enough here.

Here's the upside, annualized, you could expect at 15x P/E today. And remember, this is the higher upside.

That's less than 9% per year, even at a relatively high upside. I don't believe any investor should be "happy" with that return. I have reviewed the arguments for why Brown & Brown should be highly valued and premiumized, and I find them lacking. Expansion of profit is based on inorganic growth, adding interest costs and merger complexity. Dividends, which are cited as reliable, is less than a quarter of that found in a risk-free treasury bond. The projected growth is something I don't view as likely, given changing market dynamics. The asset-light model is not unique to the company, nor is it something that the company is a leader in.

So to conclude, while the company does have significant upside at the right price, I consider the current state of the business and the current state of the insurance industry, above all, to be insufficient to support the company's overall premiumization that it currently has. The company also has risks related to its specific exposure, which I will go through below in the risk section of the article.

Using a 14x P/E on a forward basis, I reach a PT of $58/share, and would not go any higher than that at this time.

In my IG, Wolf of Value, I cover other insurers similar to BRO that I consider to be attractive - as well as other interesting investments for both conservative income-oriented investors, as well as more growth-oriented investors. I also provide more succinct and clear targets and valuations, alongside personal commentary.

Risks are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

Primary risks to the company are related to its insurance exposure, especially flooding. Most of the broker peers do not have this sort of exposure, and while the company is experienced in risk mitigation and underwriting, it does not immunize Brown & Brown from downsides.

Not only that, but the company also cited a potential shift in CAT property rates (meaning catastrophe-impacted properties). Technically, the underinsured state of the market should be attractive, but the question is whether Brown & Brown will be able to come out on top in the long term here.

For now, I'm comfortable saying that I'm not comfortable with the risk/reward ratio presented or implied, and it's one of the reasons that I discount the company - and I have increased the discount after 1Q26, which I believe to be a longer-term indicator of a more challenging trend not only for BRO, but other insurers as well. I still do not consider there to be a material reason for investing in the company.

Thesis

Brown & Brown is a class-leading insurance agency/brokerage with a twist of underwriting going into flood insurance. The company has a solid historical trend of growing earnings, dividends, and rewarding shareholders with above-market returns, but it does come with a sub-par IG credit rating and a yield that's under 1%. As of 1Q26E, I do not consider the company’s position substantially improved from a valuation entry perspective. My thesis has also outperformed the market for the last year or so, and continues to be the sole "right" one in terms of negative or positive returns as of April 2026.

The only way you could view Brown & Brown as a buy here, I believe, is if the company continued to grow its earnings the same way it has done before—organically and inorganically. However, I do not believe this to be the case in the current macro and overall financial situation. In fact, my stance that the company was likely to see a significant downside and potential for underperformance has turned out to be accurate, also going into April 2026.

I'm lowering my PT to $58/share to reflect lower potential and a more challenging market. I'm at a "HOLD" here, and I do regret the price target increase that I gave the company in December, as it reflected a transitory trend.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this, and a valuation target of $58/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026. The company doesn’t fulfill enough of my fundamental criteria for investment.

data.title: Universal Corporation: Attractive Dividend And Favorable Share Price In A Declining Industry
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I rate Universal Corporation (UVV) with a Hold/Cautious Buy. The company is surely attractive given its high dividend yield of 6.12%, but the growth perspectives are highly limited by a slowing tobacco industry and a still-uncertain Ingredients segment.

The Q4 FY2026 earnings release next month will clarify if the worries about an oversupply in the industry are concrete; meanwhile, the stock price of ~$53-54 seems attractive compared to an implied fair value of ~$65.

I advise current investors in the company to hold the stock until next Q4, considering an additional investment if the share price declines more toward the $50 levels. Similarly, new investors can enter at the same price.

Business Overview

Universal Corporation has its headquarters in Richmond, Virginia. Looking just at the handled volumes, it is the largest independent B2B company commercialising leaf tobacco in the world, but not only because the company also owns a plant-based ingredients business segment, currently growing at high rates.

During the last fiscal year, the company was able to generate $2,947.3M of revenues with a 7.90% operating margin and $95.0M of net income. As anticipated, the UVV operates in two separate business segments:

The main one, the Tobacco segment, generated 88.5% of revenues in FY2025, contributing to 95.1% of the operating income. The heart of this business is represented by Universal Leaf Tobacco Company, which has the responsibility of contracting with farmers, stores, logistic companies, and the rest of the supply chain. Factories and facilities related to this segment of the business are located in more than 30 different countries around the world, especially in the US, Brazil, some parts of Southern Africa, and South Asia. The company also reports 49% equity stakes in Socotab LLC, which operates in the oriental tobacco business in countries like Bulgaria, Greece, North Macedonia, and Turkey.

The second one is the Ingredients segment, operating under the brand Universal Ingredients. It contributed to the remaining 11.5% of revenues and 4.9% of the operating income. The segment was formed thanks to the acquisition of FruitSmart in 2020, Silva International in 2022, and Shank’s Extracts, with the goal of building a unified platform operating in the Food & Beverage industry through the commercialisation of fruit and vegetable juices, concentrates, and purées. The revenues of the segment grew 9% in the last fiscal year, reaching $338.6M and generating 3.6% of operating income. The last 9 months also showed growing revenues of 7% YoY, but with a declining operating profit due to higher fixed costs related to the depreciation of new expansion investments and headwinds from the tariff policies.

Geographically speaking, the company sells in 168 different markets, with a fundamental presence in the US, which contributed to 21% of total FY2025 revenues, followed by Belgium (18%, the main European hub), China (10%), countries such as Egypt, the Philippines, Indonesia, and Germany (around 3-5%), and the remaining 161 countries grouped together, accounting for 34.4%.

The main customers of the company are represented by the biggest tobacco manufacturers in the world, such as Philip Morris, Altria, BAT, and Japan Tobacco. As a consequence, the risk related to the high revenue concentration in these brands is extremely high, as the loss of even one of these customers would dramatically affect UVV’s income statement.

Currently, the management reported 3 key strategic pillars for the company’s growth:

  • Make the overall tobacco operations as efficient as possible, maximising volume and shares in an already mature market
  • Growing the Ingredients segment through an organic and disciplined M&A strategy to widen the company’s diversification in the Food & Beverage market
  • Enhance the corporate strength and the company’s brand, improve the capital structure and the sustainability impact, and retain the human talent

Financial Results

In the table below, I summarised Universal Corporation’s financial performances from the period FY2022-FY2025.

The company reported 4 years of constant revenue growth, with an almost 40% cumulative growth in the analysed period. I would like to highlight especially the surprising 23% increase in operating margin realised in FY2024, when the company was able to leverage better cost discipline, increasing at the same time its pricing power. Net income fell in FY2025 by roughly 21% due to $14.1M of non-cash pension settlement and a $13.4M increase in the company’s interest expenses (from $66.3M to $79.6M). More specifically, this last increase was caused by heavier-than-expected funding in inventory balances and the absorption of higher SOFR rates.

The overall sales trajectory is visible in the chart below:

The main growth drivers were both prices and volume, but, focusing solely on FY2025, the average sales prices were up 12% YoY while volumes declined 4%. Nonetheless, UVV registered a 7% increase in sales, mainly attributed to the already-cited pricing power of the company in its Tobacco end markets. On the other hand, the revenues coming from the Ingredients segment grew at a 7% average annual rate, thanks to the realised organic expansion programs of FruitSmart and Silva.

On the profitability side, the operating margin of the company slipped in FY2023 due to disruptions in the tobacco supply chain but more than recovered in FY2024 thanks to higher prices and reduced SG&A. Among the main ratios, UVV’s ROE declined in both FY2024 and FY2025 due to an expansion in the denominator (the book equity, which increased due to higher retained earnings net of buybacks) and a decrease in the numerator caused by a higher effective tax rate of 26.6% in FY2025.

The following two charts provide evidence of what I introduced in the first paragraph regarding the contribution of the company’s two main segments to both sales and operating income:

The Tobacco segment still dominates the income statement in both indicators, but Ingredients is increasing, with the management underlining that it will receive higher investments in the following years.

Moving on to the capital structure of the company, Universal Corporation holds approximately $1.08B of debt at Q3 FY2026, composed of $462M of short-term notes and $617M of long-term loans. On the other hand, the company has $1.48M in shareholders’ equity, with an implied D/E ratio of 42%. Given the seasonal nature of the business and the high working capital financing required by the business, if we net out the short-term notes related to tobacco-financing activities, the D/E ratio of UVV declines to 25%.

It is fundamental to highlight the 99 years (since 1927) of unbroken dividends distributed by the company. Such dividends have also been increased for 19 consecutive years. Currently, the dividend is running at $0.82 per quarter, implying a $3.82 annualised rate. Contrary to FY2024, when UVV bought back 100k shares, no buyback activities were registered in FY2025.

What I Am Expecting From the Next Quarter

The last earnings release for Q3 FY2026 came out on Feb. 9, 2026, and the Q4 release is expected for May 27, 2026.

In Q3, UVV reported 9M revenues of $2.21B (down 2% YoY), operating income of $183M (down 3% YoY), and net income of $75.9M (down 11% YoY). These results were obviously not encouraging, especially if we look at Q3 alone, with operating income down 21% YoY due to declines in the volume of tobacco sold, combined with inventory write-downs affecting dark-air-cured tobacco leaves.

However, comments by the management remained positive, especially on full-year demand, with the company disclosing a 250M refinance in its credit facility, which will provide UVV with more liquidity at longer maturities.

Regarding Q4 FY2026, it is usually the strongest quarter for the company in terms of revenues, so I am expecting sales to come in at $~2.9B for the whole FY2026, basically flat compared to FY2025. Regarding operating income, I am counting on $235-245M, translating to diluted EPS around $4-4.30, up from the FY2025 levels of $3.78, which were impacted by the $14M in the pension settlement charge.

I am interested in any possible disclosure by the management regarding three key points:

  • The dynamics of UVV’s end markets and the status of the tobacco industry in general, since the management highlighted in Q3 the existing probability of an oversupply event, which can be temporary or structural.
  • The status of the company’s inventory after the dark-air-cured write-downs.
  • The strategy regarding the development of the Ingredients segment, which slowed down a bit during the last quarter. Understanding if this decline is caused by a temporary absorption of fixed costs or by a less responsive market will be fundamental to judging possible future trajectories.

Regarding dividends, I am expecting a slight increase, with the company extending its streak of consecutive dividend growth for the 20th year. I, personally, don’t think buybacks will be announced for this year.

Valuation

I decided to use a DCF method, together with a relative valuation (trading multiples) and a DDM, to derive an implied share price for Universal Corporation.

DCF

Following Damodaran’s approach, I built the cost of equity for the company using the unlevered sector beta levered at UVV’s D/E ratio and tax rate (0.85), combined with a blended equity risk premium of 4.23%.

Using the weighted capital structure of the company, the company’s pre-tax cost of debt from the last 10-K of 6.0%, and the FY2025 tax rate of 26.6%, I obtained a WACC of 6.26%, increased by 1% to reflect some risks I consider real for the company, such as the customer concentration and the stock’s illiquidity premium.

For the FCFF estimation, I started from the 9M FY2026’s revenues, on top of which I added the estimated revenues from the next quarter. I then modelled a 2% nominal sales growth per year, obtained by considering a 1% decline in the global cigarette consumption, offset by an increase of 0.5% in pricing power and ~3% expected growth from the Ingredients business. I assumed a recovering operating margin of 8.7% by FY2028 (for the moment, I think both the sector’s oversupply and the company’s write-offs are temporary, but my assumptions can change after the next earnings release). CapEx is assumed at $65-70M per year (comparable to FY2024 and FY2025), with related D&A of $60-66M. Terminal growth is low, assumed at 1% for a declining market.

The table below summarises the assumptions:

Adding cash ($85.2M) and subtracting debt ($1.08B) and minority interests ($42.4M), I obtained an implied share price of $62.46 per share.

The sensitivity table below shows how the share price changes with changes in the WACC and in the TGR:

The key assumptions here are the investors’ opinions regarding the status of the tobacco industry in the future: investors convinced of a declining business in a declining industry can fairly opt for a Hold or even a Sell judgment; the numbers would support their view.

I, personally, think that the industry is not a high-growth one for sure, but I don’t think it is shrinking that much to justify terminal growth rates of less than one. My DCF base case remains at $62.46 per share.

Relative Valuation

In terms of peer groups, Universal Corporation can be compared to both major tobacco companies, such as Altria, Philip Morris International, and British American Tobacco, and B2B players operating more in the agricultural processes, such as The Andersons, Bunge, and Archer-Daniels-Midland.

I built the following comparison table with multiples coming from the April 2026 consensus estimates on Yahoo Finance. I chose P/E, EV/EBITDA, P/B, and dividend yield as the most important metrics for the relative valuation.

Applying interquartile ranges to EPS, book value per share, and EBITDA, I obtained the following share price results:

The company trades below the median Tobacco company in terms of P/E ratio, with the median EV/EBITDA multiple acting as a lower bound for the valuation.

Dividend Discount Model

Given the 99-year-old dividend history of the company, applying a valuation method focused on the equity-return side of the business is appropriate.

Given the current annualised dividend of $3.28 per share and an estimated growth rate of 2.5% in the long period (based on a 2-3% average CAGR in the past 10 years), the Gordon growth model implies a $63.73 implied share price.

The current market price of $53-54 per share is implicitly assuming a cost of equity of ~10%, higher than the bottom-up cost of equity I built above.

Football Field

The chart below summarises the blended valuation for the stock, combining the DCF, trading multiples, and DDM.

The estimated share price is around $65, against the current $53-54 per share. In other words, the stock is currently trading at ~21% discount from the implied fair value.

Limits and Risks

Of course, my valuation and my thesis are exposed to some risks, some of which I have anticipated earlier in the article.

Firstly, the UVV business is highly centred around a few key customers, with more than 50% of its revenues from tobacco concentrated in 5 manufacturers. Obviously, losing even one of these customers would drastically impact the company’s revenues.

According to the Nicotine Resource Consortium (cited on pg. 38 of the company’s 2025 Annual Report), the consumption of cigarettes in the US has been falling 2%, and the company is exposed to a slow but consistent decline in the industry, which will affect the growth in revenues.

If the industry oversupply continues in the future, there will be other write-downs in the company’s inventory, with dark-air-cured tobacco being the most probable victim. As a consequence, the company’s operating income would shrink considerably.

Conclusion

I consider Universal Corporation the classic “boring, defensive, and dividend-heavy” company, but with a huge red flag determined by a slowly declining industry. The growth perspectives are not favourable, and they will largely depend on how well the Ingredients segment will perform, even if nowadays it represents too small a part of the company to be considered attractive for new investments. The dividend is appealing, but not sufficient to justify, alone, a buy rating.

I advise current investors to hold the stock, continuing to benefit from the dividend cash flow, and consider additional investments in the company if the share price declines to around ~$50. Likewise, new investors can enter at a similar target price.

I will update my position after the Q4 earnings release.

data.title: Dell's Shift Is Bigger Than The Rally Suggests
data.description:
Dell Technologies (DELL) continues to be an attractive buy for me despite the strong bull run because the story has evolved so much that the market has yet to catch up with the change. This is no longer a company whose valuation was driven by potential AI growth. With a backlog of $43 billion and AI-related order intake at $64 billion, there is no shortage of growth here, with a large portion of it baked into the equation. Paying ~14-16 times earnings at ~20%+ growth rate means that I am effectively paying market valuations for a company that enjoys extremely good visibility.

Why the Revaluation of Dell Makes Sense

It is clear why there has been a flurry of attention on Dell lately. The financial numbers that the company reported are quite compelling, with 342% y/y growth in AI-optimized servers revenue, $113.5 billion in total revenue, and an EPS of $10.30, all very good indicators. Nevertheless, none of these constitute news. The point is that Dell has made a fundamental change to its business model within less than one year. This justifies the re-rating.

The most crucial part of Dell's shift is the forward-looking change. The company had $64 billion worth of AI orders at the end of FY2026 and ended the fiscal year with a backlog of $43 billion worth of AI orders. That is not just a pipeline of orders. That is already committed demand that will turn into revenue over the course of several upcoming quarters.

That is why I find the distinction between pipeline and backlog crucial. While companies active in the AI space are valued on expectations, Dell is increasingly doing it on execution. Management is guiding towards revenues of $140 billion in FY2027 and $50 billion in AI servers. Those numbers are no longer speculative; they are backed by orders already made by Dell's clients. That cuts out one of the biggest concerns of growth investing, timing.

Why Dell Has Transitioned From Being a Cyclical Company Into Being Structural

What is crucial about Dell Technologies' business model at the moment is that it plays a much more structural role in the ecosystem. It has ceased being a mere participant and become part of the infrastructure. That positioning is far more valuable than any individual product that Dell can deliver.

In essence, Dell is helping companies like Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta to deploy their AI infrastructure. At the same time, Dell sees a rapid expansion in the number of enterprise users; the company now has over 4,000 AI clients on board.

I believe that the story is much simpler than that. Dell's core advantage is not the technical knowledge and skills to compete and succeed in such an environment. What differentiates Dell from the competitors is the ability to successfully implement the solution for a client, from the procurement of components and financing to actual deployment and maintenance.

The question that poses itself here is margins. The business of manufacturing AI servers is nowhere close to being a lucrative business, with its dependence on commodity parts, capital intensity, and falling prices. It’s actually a very real concern and one of the key reasons that I’ve always had reservations regarding Dell.

There’s some fresh news on the matter that makes things different, though. ISG operating margins reached 14.8% in Q4, and the management guides AI-related margins to remain at the 5% mark. While that does not seem particularly impressive, it becomes relevant if we account for revenue growth and potential attach rate. That is not the key issue for me. What is more important than the level of margins is the evolution of the margin mix.

As the company transitions to providing AI-centric products, it begins to include storage, networking, deployment, and services in the offer. All of them have higher margins than the products that the company sells to enterprise customers, and the margins should grow with revenue and attach rate. In other words, what we see here is an evolution of Dell's offering—moving from selling boxes to selling full-scale solutions.

How Dell Creates Shareholder Value

On one hand, the company returned $7.5 billion to shareholders in Q4 ($5 billion spent on buybacks and $2.5 billion on dividends). On the other hand, Dell is also actively investing in AI infrastructure and improving its supply chain. That is why I see the lack of downside risk as one of the advantages of the company.

While I do believe that Dell still has upside despite the recent re-rating of shares, this is no longer a speculative investment. The market has recognized the quality and visibility of growth. The company's earnings are set to grow significantly, and the revenue has already been guaranteed by commitments from clients.

Even though the company has experienced a sharp re-rating, Dell Technologies is trading close to the market multiples. If you look at a P/E of 13-15 times on FY2027-FY2028's estimated earnings, you will see that Dell is offering market-level multiples on a revenue growth of ~20%+ and a $43 billion AI backlog.

It is important to note how earnings and thus multiple compress with growth as EPS increases from ~$12.9 currently to ~$14.7 in just one year. As a result, the forward multiple will contract quite substantially, creating a built-in cushion against any multiple compression risk. Meanwhile, an EV/Sales of ~1x for a company whose product is becoming essential in AI infrastructures seems to be undervalued.

When the Mix Cracks, the Story Changes

The critical risk that I am looking at is the ability for ISG to maintain its margins. This particular thesis would hold water if Dell could keep its operating margins in the teens while at the same time growing its AI revenue. Anything lower would indicate that the company has been unable to improve its mix, making its AI servers more a function of volume than a platform play.

The memory price increases will put additional pressure on the bottom line. DRAM and NAND continue to be volatile in nature; should input costs outpace repricing, then margin erosion is bound to happen, despite the management's history of being nimble with prices.

Lastly, the composition of the backlog is important. Should all the growth become increasingly concentrated into a few hyperscalers, then that would defeat one of the key aspects of my thesis, namely, diversification.

Bottom Line

Dell Technologies has moved to becoming a structural player in AI infrastructure quicker than I expected, and I feel more optimistic as a result. The recent stock price re-rating is justified; however, upside still lies in execution. As long as Dell keeps delivering on margins and executes backlog successfully, I would be looking to invest.

data.title: KMLM Managed Futures ETF Q1 2026 Review: Crisis, Commodities, And The Case For Trend-Following
data.description:
Q1 was certainly volatile, dominated by events in the Middle East. Oil prices shot up, equity markets tumbled, and bonds sold off on inflationary fears. 2022 all over again. We have long maintained that trend-following exposures, particularly those tilted toward commodity markets that don't cut positions when the macro gets a little unglued, can offer a robust alternative to other safe-haven choices. Through this most acute period of stress, bonds and gold both fell, while trend did well, highlighting the ability to be negatively correlated in stress periods and having exposure directly to the macro areas stocks worry about. Added bonus points for the liquidity at transparent exchange pricing that allows timely rebalancing, particularly relative to the troubles in the private equity and credit space that are continuing.

Q1 Index and Sector Performance

The KraneShares Mount Lucas Managed Futures Index Strategy ETF (KMLM) seeks to track Mount Lucas Management's KFA MLM Index. The Index finished the quarter up 8.3%. Commodity (+9.2%) and Currency (-0.1%) markets contributed to results while Global Fixed Income (-1.9%) markets detracted. Interest income added 100bps. For the twelve months ending March 2026, the Index was up 9.3%. Year to date, the Index is up 8.1%.

Q1 Index and Sector Exposures

Over the course of the quarter, Commodity exposures went from net short 1% to net long 51%, Currency exposures went from net long 60% to net short 12%, and Global Fixed Income exposures went from net short 13% to net short 75%.

Q1 Review

To be sure, some of the 2022 comparisons rhyme and, on the physical oil side, are notably worse. We are seeing both a rapid reduction in available oil supply and the physical destruction of production capacity. The scale of potential disruption through the Straits of Hormuz, if sustained for months, would be catastrophic. The world consumes roughly 100 million barrels per day, with about 20% flowing from the region, and that simply cannot continue for an extended period. The workarounds currently in place are helping with a reasonable portion of the shortfall, including rerouting oil through pipelines, Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) releases, sanctions relief, increased U.S. production, and relatively high starting inventories, but if the disruption persists, price will ration demand, and that won't be pretty. Such an outcome would likely spill over into other markets. Hopefully, it doesn't come to that.

The differences versus 2022 are significant, particularly for yields. Starting rates this time are meaningfully restrictive, well removed from the near-zero levels of that period. The economy back then was also being supported by pandemic-era, stimulus-driven demand colliding with the rolling bullwhip of staggered reopening and supply chain bottlenecks. Hiking rates in that environment made sense. Markets flirting with rate hikes in the current supply shock, with the economy already slowing, seems misguided — the Bank of England can hike, but it won't do much to rebuild natural gas infrastructure 3,000 miles away.

In the commodity sector, the top three contributors were Heating Oil, Unleaded Gas, and Crude Oil, while the bottom three detractors were Wheat, Sugar, and Soybeans. In grains, prices rallied during the quarter, front-running some of the 2022 trades that worked, as higher energy prices fed into grain costs, particularly through Natural Gas, which is essential for fertilizer production. Index positioning moved long during the quarter. In metals, Gold sold off aggressively at the onset of the Iran war, trading more like a risk asset than a defensive one, but still finished the quarter in positive territory. Copper also declined as war fears pushed growth expectations lower. In the energy sector, the oil complex finished sharply higher across the board, with Index long positions benefiting. Natural Gas finished lower, helping the Index's short position. In agriculture, Live Cattle trended higher, and Sugar staged a sharp rally into quarter-end from recent lows. Major shifts in exposure during the quarter included Corn (short to long), Crude Oil (short to long), Unleaded Gas (increased long), Soybeans (increased long), and Wheat (short to long).

In the currency sector, the Australian Dollar and Japanese Yen were the primary contributors, while the remaining currencies were all modest detractors. Weaponization of the Dollar, tariffs, rising gold prices, and pressure on the Fed to lower rates had collectively driven Dollar weakness over recent quarters, with the Index building long foreign currency exposures. That sentiment shifted quickly at the onset of the war, with the Dollar strengthening and foreign currencies weakening. Notably, the Japanese Yen was the only market that never went long, its negative trend remaining intact. Major exposure shifts during the quarter occurred in the British Pound (long to short), Canadian Dollar (reduced long), Euro Currency (long to short), and Swiss Franc (reduced long).

In the global fixed income sector, the only contributor was the Japanese Government Bond (JGB), while UK Long Gilts, Canadian Government Bonds (CGB), the Euro Bund, and the U.S. Ten-Year were all detractors. The Index began the quarter with a small net short bond exposure that built to a small net long by the end of February. As the war escalated, energy prices surged, taking inflation expectations with them. Front-running 2022 dynamics once more, bonds fell rapidly, and Index positioning adjusted, ending the quarter meaningfully short. Major shifts in exposure included the CGB (increased short), UK Long Gilt (long to short), and U.S. Ten-Year (long to small short).

Net Market Exposures

Holdings are subject to change.

Index returns are for illustrative purposes only and do not represent actual Fund performance. Index returns do not reflect management fees, transaction costs, or expenses. Indexes are unmanaged, and one cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Term Definitions:

BPS: Basis points, a unit equal to 0.01% used to measure changes in interest rates or other financial percentages.

SPR: the U.S. government's emergency crude oil stockpile stored in underground salt caverns along the Gulf Coast, used to stabilize energy markets during supply disruptions.

Net Long/Short Exposure: A futures contract is an agreement to buy (long) or sell (short) an asset at a predetermined price at a specified future date. The exposure is calculated by the position size (negative if short) times the value of one contract.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Trimble - Finding Its Course To Fair Value
data.description:
Shares of Trimble (TRMB) have been down some 20% since I last covered shares in September of last year. An $82 stock at the time is now settling around the $66 mark while markets have been setting fresh highs.

This substantial underperformance normally attracts me, if not for the fact that I was puzzled to see the momentum at the time. Hence, shares have seen a pullback, but multiples are not yet very cheap, as partial AI disruption fears are real, making me hard-pressed to get involved just yet.

Other interesting corporate events, including M&A events, are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

Current Performance

In February, Trimble reported a 1% increase in fourth-quarter sales to $970 million, although organic growth rates were reported at 4%. Similar trends were seen for the full-year results, with reported sales down 3% to $3.59 billion, as organic growth rates were reported at 6%.

Driven by cost rationalization and a gradual shift to higher-margin service revenues, Trimble managed to grow adjusted operating margins by more than 2%, to 27.5% of sales, with the improvements more pronounced in the fourth quarter, topping 32% of sales.

Non-GAAP net income was reported at $3.13 per share, this number up twenty-eight cents from the year before. This, however, comes after a $0.61 per share pre-tax stock-based compensation expense, with earnings realistically seen closer to $2.70 per share if these (and their tax implications) are factored in.

Net debt is reported at $1.14 billion, that number barely surpassing the $1.05 billion adjusted EBITDA number, for a leverage ratio just over 1 times. This excludes about $610 million worth of equity investment, as held on the balance sheet.

For 2026, the company guided for full-year sales to advance by some 7-8% to $3.86 billion, plus or minus fifty million. Non-GAAP earnings are seen up to $3.52 per share, plus or minus ten cents, as this should likely translate into realistic earnings north of $3 per share here. Trading at $66, shares trade at a realistic 22 times earnings multiple, albeit with modest leverage employed on the balance sheet here.

Where Do We Come From?

In September, I concluded that Trimble was navigating North as the market was upbeat on Trimble's transition towards recurring revenues and subscription models, boosting predictability and the margin profile of the business. This transformation included a complicated joint venture with AGCO (AGCO) as well as an acquisition of Transporeon, yet no clear direction was seen in the reported numbers here.

Investors were too focused on the GPS and R&D powerhouse, with applications used in agriculture, construction, geospatial, and transportation. The business has failed to deliver on its promises and positioning in the past, and as organic growth appeared last year, investors were too upbeat in my view, despite the right positioning. This includes products like positioning, sensing, 3D modeling, digital twins, etc.

Having seen subpar revenue growth in recent years, in part driven by a transition to a SaaS model, the company took some decisive steps in recent years. This included a EUR 1.9 billion deal to acquire German-based Transporeon, giving the business a foothold in the transportation sector. The company furthermore attributed its agriculture segment to AGCO in exchange for a $2 billion cash payment and a 15% equity ownership stake in the business.

Pegging realistic earnings close to $2.50 per share in September, an $82 stock was valued at a mid-thirty times earnings multiple. Since September, shares have been down 20%, while the realistic earnings power improved by half a dollar, reducing the earnings multiple a great deal to the low twenties.

What Now?

Over the past five years, Trimble has grown sales by around 10%, but the real transition is that the share of recurring revenues rose from about 40% of sales to 65% of sales, while non-GAAP margins improved some 5% to 28% of sales.

These are solid achievements, as the company is operating in difficult end markets. Construction makes up 45% of sales, as the sector is hit hard due to higher interest rates here, as the same goes for 15% of exposure to architects. Other key end markets include engineering and owners.

Frankly, such a re-rating is quite attractive, although the valuations were far too demanding in September, while the business performance has been very sound. This is encouraging from an operating momentum perspective, although some investors likely have fears about AI disrupting some part of the business here, but that might be an overreaction to momentum on the upside seen before.

Trying to accelerate its AI strategy, the company announced the acquisition of Document Crunch in April, used for construction-specific document analysis and risk management. Of course, AI has real potential in construction, allowing for prevention of costly errors, reducing administrative burdens, and infusing intelligence, as no details on the deal have been announced.

This deal is a real first tangible transaction following the AI strategy issued in November of last year, focusing on agentic AI systems, with multiple efforts seen making an impact in 2026.

Some Perspective

With shares down 20% from the highs, I find myself performing a balancing act. Shares are down substantially, and in between great operating performance, the reality is that earnings multiples have fallen by about a third. This is compelling, with shares now back to levels since the spring of last year, still down substantially from a near $100 mark in 2021.

So on the one hand, I am appalled at the situation for a setback and, frankly, very sound operating momentum now seen in the realistic earnings numbers as well. However, shares still trade at a premium while some argue that Trimble might be susceptible to AI disruption pressures, at least for some part of its business.

The potential for AI disruption, Trimble's AI strategy & acquisition, pace of organic growth, and margin gains are key items to look forward to when the first quarter results are to be released in the first week of May.

The current setback in the share price and solid momentum in 2025 make me me upbeat, yet cautious at the same time as shares still have seen solid gains on a net basis compared to a few years ago.

For now, I am performing a balancing act of being cautious, waiting for more clues about the 2026 performance and/or a further pullback in the share price here. With realistic earnings seen around $3 per share, I am not willing to pay a premium to get involved, but market-equivalent multiples in the high-teens should provide appeal at levels in the mid-fifties.

data.title: AppLovin: The Only Software Company I'm Buying
data.description:
Introduction

The so-called “SaaSpocalypse,” driven by fear of autonomous AI agents, has led to significant drawdowns in software companies. The iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) is trading at almost 40% below its 52-week high. While I am bearish on certain software companies and do consider there to be a real risk of AI disruption, I also believe that many high-quality companies have been unjustifiably caught up in this broad sector-wide drawdown.

I am initiating coverage on AppLovin Corporation (APP) with a Strong Buy rating. I believe the market’s current hesitation is related to the previously mentioned SaaSpocalypse and broader sector-wide concerns regarding competitive encroachment from companies like Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL). My investment thesis on APP hinges on a growth-conducive market backdrop, total addressable market expansion, superior capabilities compared to competitors, and an extraordinary growth profile available at a valuation that implies significant mispricing.

Market Backdrop

Smartphone penetration rates are rising—they reached 91% in the US in 2025, up from 85.5% in 2021. This has created a favorable environment for in-app advertising, which now accounts for the majority of mobile ad spend compared to mobile web alternatives.

When looking at the in-app spending market, it is evident that there has been considerable growth in the last few years. In particular, within the non-gaming segment, which has recently exceeded the gaming segment. Total consumer spend on in-app purchases is over $160 billion in 2025, representing an 11.1% year-over-year increase.

I believe one of the most significant trends within the broader advertising industry to be the decline of traditional TV broadcasting. With the rise of Connected TV (CTV) and streaming services, the growth of typical TV broadcasting has waned. US CTV advertising spend is set to grow from $33 billion in 2025 to $38 billion in 2026. This transition from broadcasting to streaming & CTV is exacerbated by the creation of lower-priced subscription tiers, which are financially supported by advertising revenue by streaming services like Netflix, Inc. (NFLX), and The Walt Disney Company’s (DIS) Disney+.

The relevance for APP is in the monetization gap that currently exists in CTV. While streaming now accounts for 41% of total TV viewing time, it attracts only 7.7% of total ad spend. This discrepancy provides an opportunity for platforms like APP that can bring mobile advertising-style performance measurement to TV. Furthermore, the rise of Free Ad-Supported Streaming TV (FAST) channels represents the fastest-growing segment of the market - over 125 million people in the US are expected to use a FAST service at least once a month in 2026.

AppLovin is a Key Infrastructure Layer for the Mobile App Economy

Rather than competing as a participant in the advertising auction, APP provides the venue, the engine, and the measurement tools that facilitate the entire ecosystem. This infrastructure-level positioning allows APP to capture value regardless of which specific apps are gaining popularity.

APP’s software platform is built on three core pillars that work in a complementary feedback loop.

  • MAX is the supply-side mediation platform that allows developers to monetize their apps via automated auctions. MAX allows publishers to receive the highest possible price by ensuring all demand sources compete simultaneously for every impression.
  • AppDiscovery & AXON 2.0 represent the demand-side platform. This is the engine that drives user acquisition for advertisers. AppDiscovery uses the AXON 2.0 machine learning engine to predict which users are most likely to provide a high lifetime value for a given app.
  • Adjust provides a subscription-based layer of analytics that allows marketers to verify the performance of their campaigns.

This positioning creates a moat: APP has a large amount of data on the outcomes of in-app auctions. Because it operates both the auction (MAX) and the primary bidding engine (Axon), it can bridge signals between supply and demand to optimize for a higher return on ad spend. Many competitors, in particular DSPs and smaller mediation layers that only operate on one side of the stack, lack this full-stack visibility. This moat is one of the reasons why I believe the extension of the software sector-wide selloff to APP is unjustifiable.

While GOOG dominates the Android ecosystem and META has control over cross-app user tracking, APP has shown excellent performance in targeted mobile user acquisition, particularly in high-intent gaming and now e-commerce environments. According to Northbeam, APP delivers a 45% higher return on ad spending than META.

Exceptional Growth Profile

In the context of the Rule of 40, APP is currently delivering a score of 150 (66% revenue growth plus 84% adjusted EBITDA margin in Q4 2025). Most top SaaS companies don’t maintain a score above 50. APP has a Seeking Alpha Growth Grade of A-, showing much higher levels of growth than sector medians, as shown above. Furthermore, this growth is not just market expansion but rather a result of market share gains and technological improvements that have enhanced the APP’s monetization.

Looking towards the future, there are several levers for growth. Recently, APP announced a partnership with Stagwell Inc. (STGW) to integrate the Axon platform into Stagwell’s media offerings, giving their clients direct access to over 1 billion daily mobile users.
